description.en: Understand tokens as the billing and capacity unit of frontier AI, how context windows shape architecture, and how per-token pricing, caching, batching and tiering combine into a real cost model.

story_variants.en:
## The Currency of AI

Frontier AI has one universal currency: the **token**. Roughly three-quarters of an English word, tokens are how Claude, GPT and Gemini all measure input size, output size, capacity limits and — critically — your bill. An enterprise that cannot reason about tokens cannot forecast AI spend, and finance teams have learned this the hard way.

> **Callout:** Nobody in your company will ask "how many tokens did we use?" They will ask "why is the AI bill 4x last month?" Token literacy is how you answer.

## Tokens In, Tokens Out — Priced Differently

All three vendors price API usage per token, with two headline numbers:

- **Input tokens** — everything you send: system prompt, conversation history, retrieved documents, tool definitions, images (converted to token equivalents).
- **Output tokens** — everything the model generates, typically priced **several times higher** than input across all vendors, because generation dominates compute cost.

Reasoning models add a twist: their internal "thinking" tokens are usually **billed as output** even when you never see them. A hard math question can quietly cost many times a simple one on the same model. Budget for it.

## Context Windows: Capacity, Not Free Real Estate

A model's **context window** is the maximum tokens it can consider at once — prompt plus history plus documents plus its own output. The landscape as of 2025-2026:

- Claude and OpenAI's mainstream models operate in the hundreds-of-thousands-of-tokens class, with some variants reaching around the million mark.
- Gemini made **very long context** (a million-plus tokens) its signature capability, enabling whole-codebase and multi-hour-transcript workloads.

Three realities temper the excitement:

1. **You pay for every token in the window, every call.** Stuffing a 500-page manual into each request is architecture by brute force — and a brute-force bill.
2. **Quality is not uniform across the window.** Models attend unevenly to the middle of very long inputs; "it fits" is not "it works." Test retrieval-style vs synthesis-style tasks separately.
3. **Latency grows with input size.** Long-context calls can be dramatically slower.

This is why **retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)** did not die when long context arrived: fetching the right 5,000 tokens usually beats shipping 500,000, on cost, latency *and* accuracy. Long context and RAG are complements — long context raises the ceiling for what you can pack when you truly need cross-document synthesis.

## The Pricing Levers Every Vendor Offers

Exact prices change too often to memorize — learn the *levers* instead:

| Lever | How it works | Typical impact |
|---|---|---|
| Model tier | Smaller models cost an order of magnitude less | Largest single lever |
| Prompt caching | Reused prompt prefixes (system prompts, docs) are billed at a steep discount | Big win for chat & agents |
| Batch processing | Async jobs with relaxed latency get discounted rates | Great for offline pipelines |
| Output limits | Cap max output tokens per call | Bounds worst-case cost |
| Context discipline | Trim history, summarize, retrieve instead of stuff | Compounds every call |

All three vendors offer some version of caching and batch discounts; the mechanics differ (cache lifetimes, minimum prefix sizes, how discounts stack), so read the current docs rather than assuming parity.

> **Adoption pitfall:** The silent budget killer is **conversation history growth**. A naive chat implementation resends the entire transcript every turn, so cost grows quadratically with conversation length. Fixes: truncate old turns, summarize them, and lean on prompt caching for the stable prefix.

## Building a Cost Model

A credible forecast needs only four estimates per workload: requests per month, average input tokens, average output tokens, and the model tier. Then:

`monthly_cost = (requests × avg_in / 1M) × input_price + (requests × avg_out / 1M) × output_price`

Run this per candidate provider and tier, then layer on caching and batching assumptions. The comparison usually surprises people: the cheapest provider for a chat workload may be the priciest for a long-document workload, because input-vs-output price ratios differ. Never compare providers on a single headline number.

Subscription products complicate the picture: ChatGPT Enterprise, Claude for Enterprise and Gemini for Workspace charge **per seat**, not per token. Seat pricing is predictable but decouples cost from usage — great for adoption, useless for API-style unit economics. Most enterprises end up with both: seats for humans, tokens for systems.

## What You'll Build

In the challenge you will implement a deterministic token-cost planner: given a monthly workload and three mock providers' per-million-token rates, compute each provider's bill, find the cheapest, and report the savings versus the most expensive. It is the same arithmetic your finance partner will ask you to defend — better to master it on mock numbers first.

Keep three takeaways: tokens are both capacity and currency; long context is a powerful tool with a bill and quality caveats attached; and the big cost levers — tiering, caching, batching, context discipline — are portable skills across Claude, OpenAI and Gemini alike.

description.en: Tokens drive both capacity (context window) and cost (input/output rates, moderated by caching).

left.label: Stuff every document into the context window each call

right.label: Retrieve the relevant slice; use long context only when synthesis demands it

description.en: Long context raises the ceiling; retrieval keeps cost, latency and accuracy sane for everyday calls.

animation_sequence.code:
in_tok, out_tok = 20_000_000, 2_000_000
rate = {"input": 3.0, "output": 15.0}
cost = (in_tok / 1e6) * rate["input"] \
     + (out_tok / 1e6) * rate["output"]
print(f"${cost:.2f}")

explanation.en: Estimate monthly input and output token volume — the two numbers finance will ask about.
Rates are per million tokens; output is typically several times input across all vendors (mock values here).
Convert volumes to millions and multiply by each rate.
Format consistently — cost reports must be reproducible to the cent.

description.en: The same workload priced across three mock providers — the comparison that anchors every provider negotiation.
The cost-optimization ladder, in the order most teams should climb it.

instruction.en: Build a token-cost budget planner across three mock providers. Complete monthly_cost(in_tokens, out_tokens, rates) to return (in_tokens / 1_000_000) * rates['input'] + (out_tokens / 1_000_000) * rates['output']. Using WORKLOAD (20,000,000 input and 2,000,000 output tokens per month), print EXACTLY four lines: one '<provider>: $<cost>' line per provider in the order provider_a, provider_b, provider_c with costs formatted to 2 decimal places, then a final line 'cheapest=<provider> saves $<amount> vs <most expensive provider>' where amount is (most expensive cost - cheapest cost) formatted to 2 decimal places. This is the exact arithmetic behind every provider cost comparison your finance team will request.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
PRICING = {  # mock per-1M-token rates for training purposes
    "provider_a": {"input": 3.0, "output": 15.0},
    "provider_b": {"input": 2.0, "output": 8.0},
    "provider_c": {"input": 1.0, "output": 4.0},
}

WORKLOAD = {"in_tokens": 20_000_000, "out_tokens": 2_000_000}

def monthly_cost(in_tokens, out_tokens, rates):
    # TODO: return the total monthly cost in dollars
    pass

# TODO: compute the cost for each provider in PRICING order
# TODO: print "<provider>: $<cost:.2f>" for provider_a, provider_b, provider_c
# TODO: print "cheapest=<provider> saves $<diff:.2f> vs <most expensive provider>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
provider_a: $90.00
provider_b: $56.00
provider_c: $28.00
cheapest=provider_c saves $62.00 vs provider_a

en: Store results in a dict {provider: cost}; find cheapest with min(costs, key=costs.get) and most expensive with max(costs, key=costs.get).
Use f-strings like f"{name}: ${cost:.2f}" and f"cheapest={lo} saves ${costs[hi]-costs[lo]:.2f} vs {hi}".

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert monthly_cost(20_000_000, 2_000_000, PRICING["provider_a"]) == 90.0
assert monthly_cost(20_000_000, 2_000_000, PRICING["provider_b"]) == 56.0
assert monthly_cost(20_000_000, 2_000_000, PRICING["provider_c"]) == 28.0
assert monthly_cost(0, 0, PRICING["provider_a"]) == 0.0
assert monthly_cost(1_000_000, 0, PRICING["provider_c"]) == 1.0

q.en: Why do all three vendors price output tokens higher than input tokens?

en: To discourage long answers for safety reasons
Generation dominates inference compute cost compared to processing input
Output tokens are larger in bytes

q.en: A reasoning model answers a hard question. Why can it cost far more than a simple question of the same visible length?

en: Hard questions are billed at a premium rate class
Internal 'thinking' tokens are typically billed as output even though you may never see them
Vendors round up costs on difficult topics

q.en: Why did RAG remain essential even after million-token context windows arrived?

en: Long context is only available on-premises
RAG is required by data-privacy law
Retrieving a relevant slice usually beats shipping everything on cost, latency and often accuracy

q.en: What makes naive chat implementations grow quadratically in cost with conversation length?

en: The full transcript is resent as input every turn, so each turn pays for all previous turns
Models charge per user message, squared
Prompt caches expire after every message
